observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to prepare a complete set of FS for each of the City’s economic enterprises which shall be attached to the consolidated FS of the General Fund in order to provide adequate information on the financial position and performance of the enterprises.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the City Accountant promptly take up necessary adjusting entries for the noted book reconciling items to reflect the correct balances of pertinent accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management instruct the GSO and concerned accountable officers/end-users and offices/departments to conduct the complete inventory of all supplies and materials at the end of the year and to prepare the corresponding report certified correct by the Inventory Committee.

We also recommended that the GSO maintain updated supplies stock cards and for the Accounting and Property Offices to reconcile their records and take up the necessary adjustments to ensure that the Inventory accounts are properly stated in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management direct the Accountant to:
Reconcile subsidiary records against the PPE ledger balances to ascertain the validity and accuracy of the account balances and ensure fair presentation thereof in the Financial Statements;
Maintain complete, updated and properly accomplished PPELCs for each class of PPE, listing therein the data as to its acquisition, description, custody, useful life and other necessary information; and
Furthermore, we recommended that Management follow the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances and direct the Inventory Committee to complete the inventory taking of all PPE of the City Government, prepare the Inventory Count Form, and upon completion of the physical count, prepare the RPCPPE.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the City Accountant to coordinate with the City Engineering Office in determining the actual status of all reported on-going projects and to effect the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts based on the result of validation in order to reflect the accurate balance of the CIP accounts;
Instruct the City Accountant to ensure that all completed and paid projects are immediately classified to their appropriate PPE accounts and to compute for the required depreciation thereof; and
Observe proper planning and coordination to avoid complications during the project implementation and ensure that infrastructure projects are completed on time.

We recommended that the LCE require the GSO the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties, and upon his application, be inspected and appraised by the concerned Appraisal Committee. If found valueless or unusable, the same shall be destroyed in the presence of the inspecting officer; if found valuable, the properties shall be sold at public auction to the highest bidder in accordance with Section 379 of R.A. No. 7160. Also, upon disposal, the carrying amount of the PPE shall be derecognized or dropped from the books.

We recommended that Management make the proper markings ‘For Official Use Only” in all the government-owned vehicles and expedite registration of the same to LTO for the issuance of government (red) plate to avoid disallowance in audit. Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular 75-6 dated November 7, 1975.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Accountable Officers to liquidate/settle their outstanding cash advances without delay by submitting the duly accomplished liquidation reports on or before the prescribed period; and
Issue demand letters to concerned officers and employees who incurred delays in the settlement.

We recommended that Management promptly file an application to increase the fidelity bond of the Disbursing Officers whose maximum accountability have already increased to ensure adequate protection of government funds in compliance with the Revised Schedule of Premium Rates or Annex C of Treasury No. 02-2009.

We recommended that the City Accounting Office attach to the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs), in addition to the Obligation Budget Request (OBRs), documentary evidence that the goods or services were delivered to, or received by, the City that would warrant the recording of Accounts Payable, such as but not limited to, certified true copies of delivery receipts, Inspection and Acceptance Reports, Statement of Work Accomplished and Progress Billings, in order to establish that the recorded obligations arose from valid claims.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to determine the validity of the unliquidated obligations or Accounts Payable amounting to ₱13,599,484.87 which are outstanding for more than two years and if found to have no legitimate claim against the City, cause the reversion of the same to the unappropriated surplus.

We recommended that Management require the concerned offices/departments to implement immediately the various PPAs funded by the NGAs, if still on-going and warranted. Otherwise, remit to the source agencies or to the Bureau of the Treasury, whichever is appropriate, the unexpended balance of the trust funds.

We recommended that the City Accounting Office prepare journal entry debiting Due from Local Government Units in the amount of ₱720,000.00 representing the fund transfers for the CY 2019 and prior years’ barangay augmentation fund and crediting Prior Period Adjustment by the same amount, in order to accurately present the receivables of the City from the barangays.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management direct the concerned offices to hasten the implementation of the partially and unimplemented 20% LDF PPAs appropriated in CYs 2020, 2021 and 2022 to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the City constituents and the general public.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the City Accountant to secure the lacking supporting documents to the DVs from the concerned offices/personnel and submit promptly to the Auditor, otherwise, the non-submission thereof would warrant the issuance of a Notice of Suspension/Disallowance; and
Require strict compliance by the City Accountant with pertinent provisions of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 to protect the interest of the government and prevent unwarranted disbursements of public funds.

We recommended that Management require all transfer of funds to Barangays for the implementation of projects and programs, be covered by a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) and other documentary requirements as enumerated in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish proper accountability and monitoring of the proper utilization of funds.
We also recommended that Management instruct the City Engineering Office to include in the Report on Government PPA, the development projects funded through the Barangay Augmentation Fund/20% DF and conduct a periodic monitoring on the status of implementation thereof and submit report to the CPDO copy furnished the City Accountant and City Budget Office.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management provide the Auditor copies of perfected contracts for infrastructure projects and its complete supporting documents within five working days from execution thereof to allow for a systematic, timely and effective review process.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management instruct the BAC to adopt the required forms of the Philippine Bidding Documents to be provided to the prospective bidders as prescribed in the GPPB Circular No. 04-2020 and ensure that all the mandatory provisions are stated in the bidding documents submitted by the bidders upon evaluation thereof.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management require the BAC Secretariat to strictly comply with the posting requirements of Notices to Proceed and approved contracts on procurement undertaken through public bidding as required by Sections 37.1.6 and 37.4.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and ensure that copies of such postings are attached to the contracts and disbursement vouchers as supporting documents.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management, henceforth, to integrate the cost of the Construction and Safety Health Program into the total construction cost of the projects to be implemented by the City, and instruct the BAC to require from the contractors the submission of the CSHP duly approved by the DOLE.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management refrain from issuing the Certificate of Acceptance simultaneously with the Certificate of Completion, disregarding the prescribed defects liability period where the City is financially protected from defects and third party liabilities, instead, shall issue the Certificate of Final Acceptance on completed projects after the one year defects liability period, after all defects/deficiencies, if any, shall have been repaired by the Contractor to the satisfaction of the Inspectorate Team and upon the submission of the Warranty Certificate by the Contractor pursuant to Section 62.2.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive inform the Office of the Supervising Auditor and the Audit Team Leader within ten days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity of any postings of project signboards and/or public notice for validation by the Audit Team.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management direct the CDRRM Officer, in coordination with the City Budget Officer and the City Accountant, to incorporate in the LDRRMFIP list of all the PPAs to be funded out of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF of previous years, for approval of the LDRRM Council in accordance with RA No. 10121, NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 and furnish the same to the Audit Team.

We commended Management for having utilized 83 per cent of the 70% Mitigation Fund for CY 2022. We, however, reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management, through CDRRMO, continue to ensure the efficient and effective utilization of the DRRMF thru implementation of programs and projects designed to avoid, lessen or limit the potential adverse impact of disasters, such as projects pertaining to disaster prevention, mitigation and preparedness.

We recommended that Management continue its implementation of the vaccination and booster dose program and widen the scope of its vaccine information drive to strengthen its constituents’ knowledge and protection against hospitalization and death from COVID-19.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the LSB require the necessary, proper, and timely data and information from concerned officials/employees to come up with the SEF plan and budget that is compliant with Sections 4 and 5 of the DepEd. DBM, and DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.

Further, we recommended that Management/LSB be provided with complete, timely and accurate reports, data, information as basis in the preparation of budget estimates.

Furthermore, we recommended that recipient schools of PPAs be identified in the plan and budget, SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1 and LSN Resolution authorizing the appropriation in compliance with Section 5.2.2 of the  DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017 that the budget shall be presented by program, activity and project (PAP), by allotment class and per school.

We recommended Management and LSB direct all concerned to:
Stop the practice of disbursing claims that do not fall under the categories enumerated in DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017, Nos. 1 and 2, s. 2020, thus, not allowed;
Request from GSP Legazpi City liquidation report for the fund transfers/cash advances totaling ₱78,500.00 and to discontinue granting fund transfers that is contrary to laws, rules and regulations; and
Present the necessary documents to support the refund of tax payment amounting to ₱10,518.36.

We recommended that Management revisit the GAD PPAs to consider prioritizing realizable programs adaptable to the current needs to optimally utilize allocated funds and commitments to gender equality and GAD goals.

We recommended the timely preparation and submission of the GAD Accomplishment Report (AR) in compliance with the set guidelines. Further, we recommended that the Audit Team be furnished copy of the GAD AR and the required accompanying reports  for verification on the judicious use, efficient and effective implementation of plan and budget in addressing gender issues.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management, through the GAD Focal Person, spearhead the updating and maintenance of the GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning,programming and policy formulation to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on the relevant data for proper identification of existing gender issues and ensure that approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GPB are gender responsive.

We commended Management for having reorganized the GFPS in September 2022, we however recommend that Management direct that GFPS Profile Form for the newly organized GFPS be accomplished to facilitate determination/evaluation on who among the members need training and what GAD capacity building programs needs to be undertaken. Henceforth, require that GFPS to attend/undergo trainings to advance the GAD advocacy of the City. The annual GAD Plan and Budget of the City should consistently include capacity building for the GFPS members.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management strictly adhere to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and 2016-01 that LGUs shall deepen knowledge and build competence on gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD related tools, among others.

Likewise, follow procedures in the implementation and management, monitoring and evaluation of the gender-responsiveness of projects to support the HGDG,PIMME/FIMME as tools in identifying programs and projects attributed to GAD budget for efficiency in identifying the issues, analysis, and monitoring its effectiveness.

We recommended Management to direct all concerned on the effective/full implementation of the following:
Inclusion of the remaining nine (9) barangays in the City’s garbage collection and disposal services and/or provide appropriate SWM assistance; and
Ecological Solid Waste Management (ESWM) on the remaining four (4) barangays.
Further, we reiterated our prior year’s recommendation to conduct strict monitoring of areas in the City where indiscriminate dumping of wastes is observed and enforce regulations, including imposition of penalties for non-compliance.

We recommended that Management direct that the each targeted PPAs (using LBF Form No. 4) shall be in harmony with the AIP and that the amount of budget be specified per line item or per PPA.  Also, the description of PPAs presented in the Accomplishment Report should correspond with the AIP and the targets/plan and budget for an effective monitoring and perforamance evaluation.
We likewise recommended for the concerned department to reconcile with the Budget and Accounting Units the actual cost/expenses incurred in the implementation of each PPAs to acurately present in the Accomplishment Report.

We recommended that Management:
Require the City Budget Office to render a systematic and realistic revenue forecasting taking into account prior years’ experiences and statistics as aid in setting targets;
Direct the City Budget Officer to undertake a review of the present budgeting done under the stewardship of the City Budget Office to set a clearer guide for the concerned revenue generating Departments toward attaining the targets set; and
Require the City Treasury and other Departments involved in generating revenues to meet its targets by conducting periodic review of collection performance by the concerned officials to ascertain whether collection targets are attained and to analyze the reasons for the shortfall in collections and find alternative solutions to minimize, reduce or avoid incurrence of shortfall in the future.

We commended Management for achieving high collection efficiency on locally sourced revenues, having significant gains in the revenue generation during the year; however, we recommended that Management continue the conduct of periodic review of accomplishments in order to have assurance that all revenue sources are tapped to maximize its revenue generating powers as mandated by law.

We recommended that Management direct the City Legal Office to take appropriate legal action on the rental delinquency amounting to ₱3,200,000.00, that may include the collection of interests and surcharges, among others.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team, written justification as to the exclusion of the ₱3,050,179.92 Franchise Tax from the Notice of Assessment due from the power and energy corporation, despite its non-collection.
We further recommended that Management take appropriate/legal action on the continued failure of the power and energy corporation on the payment of its franchise tax despite final demand of the City, which may include the collection of interests, penalties and litigation costs, among others.

We recommended that Management cause the immediate refund of ₱38,093,785.73 representing the amount paid to officials and employees of the City Government of Legazpi, considering that the said disbursement of funds is irregular due to absence of any laws, rules and regulations entitling government officials and employees to receive such kind of benefits.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with Section 261(v) of the Omnibus Election Code, specifically refrain from disbursing, releasing or expending public funds declared as prohibited acts, within 45 days before the regular election, to avoid irregular expenditures which may result to the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

Ensure that the total amount of the approved APP, including Supplemental APPs, tally with the final budgetary allocation of the City for the year.

We recommended that Management provide the Auditor copies of perfected contracts for infrastructure projects and its complete supporting documents within five working days from execution thereof to allow for a systematic, timely and effective review process.

We recommended that henceforth, Management to instruct the BAC to adopt the required forms of the Philippine Bidding Documents to be provided to the prospective bidders as prescribed in the GPPB Circular No. 04-2020 and ensure that all the mandatory provisions are stated in the bidding documents submitted by the bidders upon evaluation thereof.

We recommended that Management require the BAC to secure copy of the required valid and notarized Joint Venture Agreement, SLCC and Owners’ Certificate of Acceptance from the winning contractors on the aforementioned infrastructure projects and henceforth instruct the BAC Secretariat to check the completeness the bidding documents submitted by the bidders to ensure full compliance with the eligibility criteria set in the GPPB Circular and 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that Management require the BAC Secretariat to strictly comply with the posting requirements of Notice of Award, Notice to Proceed and approved contract on procurement undertaken through public bidding as required by Sections 37.1.6 and 37.4.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and ensure that copies of such postings are attached to the contracts and disbursement vouchers as supporting documents.

We recommended that Management, henceforth, integrate the cost of the Construction and Safety Health Program into the total construction cost of the projects to be implemented by the City, and instruct the BAC to require from the contractors the submission of the CSHP duly approved by the DOLE.

We recommended that Management refrain from issuing the Certificate of Acceptance simultaneously with the Certificate of Completion, disregarding the prescribed defects liability period where the City is financially protected from defects and third party liabilities, instead, shall issue the Certificate of Final Acceptance on completed projects after the one year defects liability period, after all defects/deficiencies, if any, shall have been corrected by the Contractor to the satisfaction of the PE and upon the submission of the Warranty Certificate by the Contractor pursuant to Section 62.2.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant promptly take up necessary adjusting entries for the noted book reconciling items to reflect the correct balances of pertinent accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management direct:
The City Treasurer to send written notices to the payees of unclaimed checks at least one month before a check becomes stale;
The City Accountant review the long outstanding stale checks and take up the necessary entry reversing to Cash-in Bank and Accounts Payable the amounts thereof; and
The City Treasurer to cancel the unclaimed stale checks and furnish the accountant with the original disbursement vouchers (DV) and its supporting documents, for the preparation of the Journal Voucher/s following the procedures set forth in the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Accountable Officers to liquidate/settle their outstanding cash advances for travel without delay by submitting the duly accomplished liquidation reports on or before the prescribed period; and
Issue demand letters to concerned officers and employees who incurred delays in the settlement of their cash advances.

We recommended that Management require the City Treasurer to identify the specific sources of funds and determine whether these were idle funds in accordance with Sections 21 and 22 of COA Circular No. 92-382. In case when these are idle funds, investing the same should have with prior authority from the SP and the approval by the Local Chief Executive.
Further, we recommended that Management transfer the ₱56.04 million to the City’s Current Account maintained for Trust Funds to hasten the implementation of the programs, projects and activities for which the fund was created/intended.

We recommended that the City Accountant prepare a Request for Authority to Write-off the dormant receivable account amounting to ₱0.73 million following the procedures provided in Sections 8.2 and 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
We also recommended that the City Accountant provide adequate Allowance for Impairment on the Other Receivables which are deemed to be uncollectible.

We recommended that the CAO take up the unliquidated fund transfers to the Barangays for the implementation of various barangay infrastructure projects as Due from LGUs under the Asset account and the reversing entry of Accounts Payable (unreleased fund) for proper monitoring and fair presentation in the financial statement. Henceforth, refrain from expensing outright the fund transfers for a specific purpose for which timely liquidation is required.
Management Comment:
The City has been treating the barangay augmentation as Subsidy to lessen the required documentations from barangay in order to ease the release and implementation of projects. The required liquidation and completion of projects prior to the release of next augmentation are control mechanism to follow responsibility and accountability principles. However, Management promised to comply with the audit recommendation and such shall be discussed with the Liga ng mga Barangay.
Copy of the adjusting entry was submitted by the City Accountant during the exit conference, on Accounts Payable amounting to ₱5.20 million per JEV No. 2102004A-18 dated February 28, 2021.

We recommended that Management instruct the Accounting and General Services Office to follow the Perpetual Inventory Method and recognize as inventories purchases of supplies and materials for stock, regardless of whether or not they are consumed within the accounting period.
Henceforth, strictly enforce compliance with the proper procedures on the receipt, issuance and recording of supplies and materials pursuant to the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that Management instruct the GSO and concerned accountable officer/end-user offices/departments to conduct the inventory of all supplies and materials at the end of the year and to prepare the corresponding report certified correct by the Inventory Committee.
We also recommended that the GSO maintain supplies stock cards and for the Accounting and Property Offices reconcile their records and take up the necessary adjustments to ensure that the Inventory accounts are properly stated in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management take urgent appropriate action to acquire ownership of the subject property to fully protect the interest of the City.
Management Comment:
Management informed the Audit Team that despite thorough follow-ups to the Philippine National Railways in particular with the Manila Railroad Company, until now they have not yet furnished the City with the Deed of Sale, which is repeatedly requested, it being a prerequisite of the subdivision plan.
Further, instruction to the City UPAO Coordinator is made to take urgent attention to personally gather the necessary documents from the said company to establish and secure ownership of the subject property in compliance with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the City Accountant to verify the cause/s of the discrepancy between the balances appearing in the general ledger as against the subsidiary ledgers of the CIP accounts and make the necessary correction to reconcile the same;
Require the City Accountant and the General Services Officer to reconcile the items listed in the RPCPPE and the subsidiary ledgers;
Direct the City Accountant to coordinate with the City Engineering Office in determining the actual status of all reported on-going projects and to effect the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts based on the result of validation in order to reflect the accurate balance of the CIP accounts; and
Instruct the City Accountant to ensure that all completed and paid projects are immediately classified to their appropriate PPE accounts and to compute for the required depreciation thereof.
Management Comment:
Appropriate instructions or reminder to the City Accountant and General Services Officer were issued to undertake reconciliation, necessary adjustments and coordinate with the City Engineering Office in order to make correcting entries in the books of accounts for the understatement/overstatement of PPE accounts.
Further, the City Accountant was reminded on the treatment to comply with the said COA provision on the treatment of Construction in Progress and to compute the required depreciation of assets.
During the exit conference, copy of the adjusting entry per JEV No. 2101002A-18 dated January 31, 2021 was submitted by the City Accountant on Construction in Progress amounting to ₱2.75 million.

Further, require the City Budget Officer to appropriate funds for the payment of transfer taxes and other fees so that the processing of the titles could be facilitated.
We also recommended that the Inventory Committee be more comprehensive and detailed in accomplishing the RPCPPE, particularly the Land account, by including the Title Number and Tax Declaration Number for each lot owned by the City Government of Legazpi to ensure that all land properties are well accounted for.

Management explained that properties are still in the process for transfer to the City Government of Legazpi.

Henceforth, require all departments to course through the General Services Office all donations-in-kind for inspection, acceptance, issuance of the Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE) and recording in the appropriate Property Ledger Cards; and
Direct the GSO to furnish the CAO with relevant documents for the proper recording in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the CAO reclassify and make the necessary correcting entry by debiting the Due to NGAs account in the amount of ₱35.04 million and crediting the Trust Liability- DRRM by the same amount.
We also recommended that the subject amount be incorporated in the preparation of the CY 2021 Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan, be integrated in the Annual Investment Program of the City, and be fully utilized on or before December 31, 2021, otherwise it will be reverted to the National Treasury.

Further, Management committed that the same shall be integrated in the Annual Investment Program as part of the CY 2021 Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan.

We recommended that the CAO reclassify and make the necessary correcting entry by debiting the Due to NGAs account in the amount of ₱25.17 million and crediting the Trust Liabilities account by the same amount. Henceforth, observe conscientiousness in the recording of fund transfers from other agencies to prevent misstatements in the financial statements.
We also recommended that Management fast track the implementation of the afore-cited projects to ensure effective and efficient utilization of Local Government Support Funds and the timely delivery of services for the benefit of the constituents.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned offices to hasten the implementation of the partially and unimplemented 20% LDF PPAs appropriated in CY 2020 to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the City constituents and the general public.
Management Comment:
Management explained that due to the pandemic, there was an unexpected change of priority in the implementation of programs, projects and activities in the City even in terms of focus of attention. Some PPAs were not implemented and/or appropriation not utilized were due to uncompleted processing of transactions and/or documentations.
Nonetheless, the City promised to fast track the implementation of these projects as continuing appropriations to fully attain the desired outcomes specifically for socio-economic development and environmental protection for the general welfare of the constituents of the City of Legazpi.

We recommended that Management require all transfer of funds to Barangays for the implementation of projects and programs, be covered by a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) and other documentary requirements as enumerated in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish proper accountability and monitoring of the proper utilization of funds.
Further, we recommended that the City Accountant review thoroughly the required documentation for fund transfer, require the implementing agency to submit also the liquidation and accomplishment reports to ensure that the funds are used for its intended purpose.
Furthermore, that the City Accountant take up the unliquidated fund transfers to the Barangays for the implementation of various barangay infrastructure projects as Due from LGUs under the Asset account and the reversing entry of Accounts Payable (for the unreleased fund). Henceforth, refrain from expensing outright the fund transfers for a specific purpose for which timely liquidation is required. Credit the account Due from LGUs shall be made upon liquidation of fund transfer by the Barangay.

For additional documentation specifically the execution of the Memorandum of Agreement between the barangay and the City of Legazpi intended for the fund transfer of augmentation, such shall be first presented for proper consultation, thus, Management requested that the starting time for implementation of this audit observation shall be on CY 2021 and rest assured that consultation with the Audit Team shall be made before finalization whatever the process or procedures will be adopted.

We recommended that the City Planning and Development Office furnish the Regional Offices of the DILG and DBM copies of the Annual Investment Program immediately after its approval to enable the oversight agencies to create/maintain the necessary database and to monitor the utilization of the 20 percent Development Fund.

Likewise, transmittal letters of the CYs 2020 and 2021 Annual Investment Plan are now being prepared for submission to the DILG and DBM Regional Offices to be able for the oversight bodies to create/maintain the necessary database and to monitor the utilization of the projects listed under the 20% Development Fund. Copy of the transmittal letters will be forwarded to the Audit Team once received by the concerned agencies.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the City Accountant to secure the lacking supporting documents to the DVs from the concerned offices/personnel and submit promptly to the Auditor, otherwise, the non-submission thereof would warrant the issuance of a Notice of Suspension/Disallowance; and
Require strict compliance by the City Accountant with pertinent provisions of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 to protect the interest of the government and prevent unwarranted disbursements of public funds.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management, through the GAD Focal Point System, hasten the setting up and maintenance of the GAD Database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis and ensure that approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GPB are gender responsive.
Management Comment:
The City Planning and Development Office was able to submit hard copies of GAD Database of the City on March 9, 2021, however, such is still subject to continuous updating which is in process.
Management expounded that in order to have an effective and efficient gender responsive planning, programming and policy formulation, the City is incorporating in the GPB software to include the GAD database in the overall management information system. Further, Management gave assurance that the GAD database will be the priority project of the City to be able to address not only its existing GAD issues and concerns but also the overall needs for socio-economic development of the community.

We recommended that Management establish a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation System to enable it to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of its gender mainstreaming efforts. Further, instruct the GAD Focal Person to include all M&E activities in the annual GPB.

Management explained that the plan to have the proper training or seminar regarding the monitoring and evaluation techniques and skills for GAD programs and projects did not materialize due to the pandemic, hence, the Monitoring and Evaluation System is still not in place.
Management, however, committed to include in the M&E establishment this CY 2021 to enable the City to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of its gender mainstreaming efforts.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 that LGUs shall deepen knowledge and build competence on gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD related tools, among others.
Management Comment:
Management justified that the subject programs/projects are attributed in parcel or whole of its activities to support gender-responsive PPAs, and that not all the PPAs are fully gender-responsive and gender sensitive, instead an attribution of LGU’s regular programs to the GAD Plans and Budget.
Henceforth, the City is certain to conduct capacity development activity in order to gain more skills and knowledge to have self-assessment of identifying gender-responsive PPAs upon the completion of GAD database.

We recommended that Management require City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (CDRRMO) to prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP as required under COA Circular No. 2012-002 indicating the 30 per cent allocation for Quick Response Fund and the 70 per cent allocation for Disaster Mitigation, Prevention and Preparedness with details as to the projects and activities to be funded thereof.  Also include, under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the LDRRM Special Trust Fund. Henceforth, the CDRRMO prepare and submit the same annually, furnishing a copy thereof to the Audit Team.
Management Comment:
Management greatly appreciated the observation of the Audit Team and in pursuance to the provisions of the aforementioned COA Circular, the CDRRM Officer has submitted on March 10, 2021, the required LDRRMFIP for CY 2020 using the required template, for the following allocations:
the 30% allocation for Quick Response Fund;
the 70% allocation for Disaster Mitigation, Prevention and Preparedness with details as to the projects and activities to be funded; and
the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years (LDRRM Special Trust Fund)

Said CDRRMIP approved by the CDRRM/CCAM Council on August 14, 2019 under Resolution No. 002, series of 2019 as substantiated by the Minutes of the 3rd Quarter Meeting and copies of which were submitted to the Audit Team.
Management during the exit conference admitted that some errors were inadvertently committed in the AIP.

We recommended that Management, through CDRRMO, ensure the efficient and effective utilization of the DRRMF thru implementation of programs and projects designed to avoid, lessen or limit the potential adverse impact of disasters, such as projects pertaining to disaster prevention, mitigation and preparedness.
Management Comment:
Management explained that the remaining balance of CDRRMF budget of ₱24.20 million represents appropriation for Capital Outlays particularly for Land Improvement, Other Structures and various types of equipment which were not implemented in view of the abrupt change of focus and/or priority of the CDRRMO staff on COVID-19 responses at the time of pandemic.
With the present needs of the constituents of the City to avoid adverse impact of disasters, and also to consider the identified projects of the CDRRMC, such as the construction of the City Warehouse for stockpiling of relief goods, and the procurement of various equipment, Management is determined to fast track the implementation of said projects in order to ensure the efficient and effective utilization of CDRRMF.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to formulate/prepare the required Three-Year Strategic Program and consider the recommendations of the DepED PSDS and SDS to serve as reference in the preparation of the SEF budget.
Henceforth, the LSB strictly follow the required processes and documents in planning and budgeting for SEF under the revised guidelines on the use of SEF pursuant to DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017.

Management informed the Audit Team that the SEF Budget Proposal emanates from the DepEd-Legazpi City Division and is subjected to review and evaluation of the Legazpi City School Board to be presented by the SDS. The only constraint is the non-preparation of the Three-Year Strategic Plan which should be prepared first by the DepEd-Legazpi as basis for the prioritization of programs/projects/activities in the budget preparation for SEF.
The Audit Team recommendation shall be complied to follow the required processes and documents for planning and budgeting for SEF under the revised guidelines prescribed under DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017. The LSB is now in the process of formulating/preparing the three-year strategic plan which will be submitted to the Audit Team for information and reference purposes.

We recommended that Management, in coordination with the Local School Board, ensure that the total share on the additional one (1%) per cent tax on Real Property Tax and the unexpended balances of the SEF budget from the previous year be completely appropriated in succeeding year’s SEF Annual Budget to ensure funding of priority programs, projects and activities of public schools in the City.
Management Comment:
Management reasoned out that the policy of the City in the issuance of Certification by the City Treasurer as to the estimated income for the budget year for SEF is based on the projected Real Property Tax to be collected during the budget year as to the amount presented in the City’s Annual Budget, and that any unappropriated and unexpended share of SEF will still form part of the unappropriated surplus due to SEF to be carried over to the next budget.
In cases of urgent needs for augmentation of funds and to implement unplanned programs/projects/activities, the LSB has to pass supplemental budgets, chargeable to available funds as excess collections and/or unappropriated SEF funds.
Further, Management gave assurance that the total share of SEF on the additional one (1) per cent tax on Real Property shall be properly allotted or earmarked to SEF.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
Although education is not devolved, the resources that the LGUs provide to the education sector is very significant. Determination of the special education tax collection estimate and the release of fund should at all times be in accordance with laws, rules and regulations. Income estimates provided by the City should be transparent and can be validated by the LSB and all concerned, as well as the reporting of any realized surplus at the end of the fiscal year.

We recommended that LSB strictly observe submission of the required reports to the concerned oversight department/agency and posting to the City’s website to promote transparency and accountability on the SEF.
Management Comment:
Management promised to comply with the audit recommendation.

Management Comment:
Management explained that the Approved Ten-Year Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan (ESWMP) of the City served as guide to the City Environment and Natural Resource Office towards the efficient implementation of the programs/projects/activities ensuring environmental and public health protection.
These projects implemented by the City such as the Establishment of Sanitary Landfill, the Construction of Composting Facility, and the Materials Recovery Facility of 65 barangays have showcased the best practices of the City which have been sustained.
Hence, Management is grateful for the Audit Team commendation to continue environmental protection in complying the provisions of the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 with commitment to strictly monitor the areas in the City where indiscriminate dumping of wastes is observed, and enforce regulations including imposition of penalties for non-compliance.

We recommended that the City through the DILG LGOO establish an IMTF for proper monitoring and evaluation of the LCPC functionality and submit the required report to the Provincial IMTF, copy furnished the DILG. We also recommended that Management refer to the DILG issuance on the “New Comprehensive Guidelines for the Establishment, Strengthening and Monitoring of the LCPC at All Levels and for Other Purposes” which takes effect in CY 2021 for proper guidance.
Management Comment:
With the issuance of DILG guidelines on the “New Comprehensive Guidelines for the Establishment, Strengthening and Monitoring of the LCPC at all Levels and for Other Purposes”, Management stated that the priority plan of LCPC is to reorganize Legazpi City Council for the Protection of Children (LCCPC) to be spearheaded by the DILG LGOO, and in time also for the creation of the IMTF that will monitor and evaluate the functionality of the LCCPC.
Management added that such establishment of IMTF shall be done for proper monitoring and evaluation of the LCPC functionality and submission of required reports shall be made to the Provincial IMTF, copy furnished the DILG.

We recommended that, henceforth, Management strictly adhere to the guidelines of government grants for COVID-19, giving priority to programs, projects and activities that address the pandemic, to curtail and eliminate its threat to the community.
Management Comment:
Management commented that the expenditures incurred in the amount of  ₱470,454 as mentioned in the audit observation were considered as necessary COVID-19 related PPAs and expenses under Item 3.5.12 of DBM LBC No. 125. Moreover, they appreciate the observation of the Audit Team on the charging and utilization of the BGCM with the city’s assurance and commitment to strictly adhere with the audit recommendation.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
As narrated in the audit observation the expenses totaling ₱470,454 is deemed improperly charged to the BGCM fund as they fall among the unallowable expenses under Section 3.6.3 of the Circular, thus should be charged against the General Fund of the City. Further, expenses amounting to ₱26,636 was incurred in February 7, 2020, two months before the issuance of the guidelines on the release and utilization of the BGCM. Furthermore, expenses for the printing of AICS and SAP Card could have been subsidized by the National Agency per Section 6.1 of the DSWD Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 Series of 2020 (Special Guidelines on the Provision of Social Amelioration Measures by the DSWD, DOLE, DTI, DA, DOF, DBM, and DILG xxx):
Section 6.1
The National Government Agencies (NGAs) covered in this JMC shall continue to use their own appropriated, existing, and available funds for their respective social amelioration programs, subject to the pertinent budgeting, accounting, and auditing rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management:
Compel the CSWD Officer to fast track the submission of the Relief Distribution Sheets indicating the names of recipients and their signatures, otherwise, non-submission thereof would warrant the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance; and
Require the City Accountant to ensure that all claims against government funds shall be supported with complete documentation to protect the interests of the government and to prevent unwarranted disbursements of public funds.
Management Comment:
The CSWD Office has submitted to the Audit Team copies of the Relief Distribution Sheets/Report of Commodities Released signed and received by the respective barangay officials/beneficiaries with corresponding Summary of Commodities Released on February 23, 2021, as partial submission representing 90% of the procured goods. The remaining distribution reports will be submitted as soon as the Office of the CSWD has collected the reports from Front-liners - military and health personnel.

Management Comment:
The CSWD Officer has submitted documents on the Timeline of SAP implementation in Legazpi City for information/reference and explained that the delays were due to the following reasons:
Delayed receipt and constant amendments of guidelines/circulars. DSWD’s Omnibus Guidelines MC No. 09 series of 2020 was sent only five (5) days after the receipt of funds. DSWD and DILG memoranda on additional sectors were received late also which left the barangays revising their lists now and then. The Omnibus guidelines were supposed to be the bible of the implementers, thus, its delay – particularly on the exclusion, affected the speed of the SAP implementation;
The simultaneous implementation of SAP mentioned in the JMC No. 1 series of 2020 hampered the finalization of master lists by the barangay as duplication of the services was not allowed. Some barangays waited for the DSWD’s ESP as the amount was bigger than DOLE’S TUPAD, only to move them back to DOLE upon learning that DSWD’s slots were limited. The wait took longer but the barangays wanted to ensure that those most qualified are listed in DSWD and no one is left behind.
In most cases, however, some of those listed reported that they have already received assistance from other agencies, thus, the barangays had to replace them; and
There was very limited time left to administer the SAC but the latter was needed to ensure that no one was left behind and only those qualified were listed. The SAC aided them to determine which of the residents, were expecting/have received DOLE CAMP, SSS-SWBS and DA’s cash assistance to farmers. It was the basis for the payroll list. Unfortunately, when everything had been encoded, DSWD changed the format for uploading.
Management further stated that they appreciate the observation and recommendation regarding the matter and that they are determined to sustain the best practices they have for the benefit of the Legazpeños as beneficiaries of the Emergency Subsidy Program.

We recommended that Management:
Compel the CSWD Officer to submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents for the Social Amelioration Program-Emergency Subsidy Program (SAP-ESP) of the remaining barangays;
Require the City Accountant to ensure that disbursements of funds are properly supported with the complete required supporting documents; and
In future subsidies, the CSWD Officer strictly comply with the terms and conditions stipulated in the Memorandum of Agreement to ensure that the subsidies are actually received by the targeted beneficiaries.
Management Comment:
The CSWD Officer explained that all the documents needed for SAP payout were completed by the CSWD, hence issuance of the second tranche was not hampered.
As the time element for SAP implementation was very short, the following procedures were made:
The Municipal Action Team (MAT) Leader and CSWDO uploaded to DSWD the data in the SAC forms encoded by DSWD augmentation staff. Questions from Regional Office were channeled to the MAT Leader who in turn relayed them to the staff in charge of the barangays or to the CSWDO;
Pictures of the SAP payouts were sent by the barangays directly to the DSWD email shared by the MAT Leader to the barangays and to her email. In most cases, however, barangays furnished CSWDO copies of the same. To ensure that all received barangay reports were properly accounted for, CSWDO emailed its copies to DSWD;
Pictures and list of corrections were sent through emails, not hard copies, directly to DSWD or thru the MAT Leader as CSWDO wanted to ensure that nothing was lost in the communication process. No one was allowed to post payouts in any social media for security reason.
As such, the CSWD Officer claimed that when the Audit Team requested for copies of the pictures, the CSWDO was unable to provide all as most were emailed only. However, on March 9, 2021, the CSWDO has submitted all the required documents for SAP payout by the barangays.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of actions to be undertaken by the City Treasurer:

On Revenue Accounts that Reported Decreases in Collection – 

Observe proper accounting of funds in recognizing income sources so that they could form part of the Current Year Income; and,

Implement a collection drive from servicing businesses within the City that do not pay correct taxes by requiring declaration of correct gross receipts of taxpayers including information on the delivery trucks and vans used in business operations.


On Revenue Accounts that Reported Increases in Collections – 

Intensify the collection of all revenue accounts, particularly the business taxes and Lease Income.

We further recommended the following:

Refrain from accepting application for renewal of business permits which are not based on the documentary requirements like the audited Financial Statements stamped “Received by” the BIR or VAT returns particularly when the declarants submit prior year’s gross receipts or sales in rounded figures.  When circumstance warrant, institute appropriate action against proprietors/owners of business establishments found to have not declared the correct or accurate gross receipts or sales in the prior year;

Require submission of complete financial records from taxpayers to correctly assess tax due.  Consider the possibility of establishing linkage with Bureau of Internal Revenue through modern technology in order to do away with manual intervention in the assessment of business taxes.  Likewise, the factual gross receipts or sales of the business establishments should be reflected in the application form, instead of zero.  This will facilitate the determination on the correctness of the amount of business tax paid to the City; and,

Make use of the information generated on the business establishments from the automated system of the City.  Consider fielding Inspectors to the Barangays where it is unlikely to have 50 business establishments or less to ensure that these companies/businesses are paying the required taxes due to the City.
